content.text: The basic building blocks of geometry are points, line segments, lines, and rays.

content.title: Definitions
items: **Point**: A sharp dot that determines a precise location. It has no length, breadth, or height. Labeled with a capital letter like P.
**Line Segment**: The shortest path between two points. It has two end points. Denoted as AB or BA.
**Line**: A line segment extended indefinitely in both directions. Denoted as $\overleftrightarrow{AB}$. Any two points determine a unique line.
**Ray**: A part of a line that starts at one point and goes on endlessly in one direction. Denoted as AP.

content.title: Folding a Line Segment
procedure: Fold a piece of paper and unfold it.
The crease formed gives the idea of a line segment.

content.title: Example-1
content.question: How many lines can be drawn passing through: (a) one fixed point? (b) two fixed points?
content.solution:
(a) An uncountable (infinite) number of lines can pass through one fixed point.
(b) Only one unique line can pass through two fixed points.

content.text: An angle is formed by two rays having a common starting point called the vertex. The rays are called the arms of the angle.

content.title: Naming an Angle
content.text: An angle formed by rays BD and BE is named as $\angle DBE$ or $\angle EBD$. The vertex (B) is always the middle letter.

content.title: Opening a Book
procedure: Observe the cover of a book as you open it.
Notice how the angle between the cover and the first page changes as you rotate it more.
content.observation: The size of an angle is the amount of rotation or turn needed to move the first ray to the second ray.

content.title: Example-2
content.question: Name the arms and the vertex of $\angle SRT$.
content.solution:
The vertex is the middle letter, S.
The arms are the rays SR and ST.

content.title: Classification based on Turn
items: **Right Angle**: Exactly a quarter turn ($90^\circ$). Resembles the shape of an 'L'.
**Straight Angle**: A half turn ($180^\circ$). The arms lie in a straight line.
**Acute Angle**: Sharp angle, less than a right angle ($< 90^\circ$).
**Obtuse Angle**: Blunt angle, greater than a right angle but less than a straight angle ($90^\circ < x < 180^\circ$).
**Reflex Angle**: Greater than a straight angle but less than a full turn ($180^\circ < x < 360^\circ$).

content.title: Perpendicular Lines
content.text: Two lines that meet at right angles are called perpendicular lines.

content.title: Example-3
content.question: Classify the following angle measures: $30^\circ, 90^\circ, 120^\circ, 258^\circ$.
content.solution:
- $30^\circ$: Acute (less than $90^\circ$)
- $90^\circ$: Right
- $120^\circ$: Obtuse (between $90^\circ$ and $180^\circ$)
- $258^\circ$: Reflex (between $180^\circ$ and $360^\circ$)

content.text: Mathematicians divided a circle into 360 equal parts. Each part is $1^\circ$ (one degree).

content.title: Why 360?
content.text: 360 is the smallest number evenly divisible by all numbers from 1 to 10 (except 7). Ancient texts like the Rigveda mention 360 spokes in a wheel, and ancient calendars used 360 days in a year.

content.title: Paper Protractor
procedure: Cut a circle and fold it into halves ($180^\circ$).
content.conclusion: The line that divides an angle into two equal parts is called an angle bisector.

content.text: A clock is divided into 12 equal parts. The total angle is $360^\circ$.

content.title: Example-4
content.question: What is the angle between the hands of a clock at 1 o'clock?
content.solution:
Total angle = $360^\circ$.
Number of parts = 12.
Angle between two successive numbers = $360 \div 12 = 30^\circ$.
At 1 o'clock, the hands are 1 part apart, so the angle is $30^\circ$.

content.title: Example-5
content.question: What is the angle at 4 o'clock?
content.solution:
At 4 o'clock, the hands are 4 parts apart.
Angle = $4 \times 30^\circ = 120^\circ$.

glossary.definition: A precise location denoted by a capital letter, having no dimensions.
The shortest path between two end points.
A part of a line with one starting point, going endlessly in one direction.
The common starting point of two rays forming an angle.
An angle smaller than a right angle (more than $0^\circ$ and less than $90^\circ$).
An angle greater than a right angle but less than a straight angle.
An angle greater than $180^\circ$ but less than $360^\circ$.
A line that divides an angle into two equal smaller angles.

summary_points: Points, lines, segments, and rays are the building blocks of plane geometry.
An angle measures the amount of rotation between two arms about a vertex.
Angles are measured in degrees using a protractor, with $360^\circ$ representing a full rotation.
Special angles include right angles ($90^\circ$) and straight angles ($180^\circ$).
Angles can be compared using superimposition and categorized into acute, obtuse, and reflex based on their size.
Real-life applications of angles include clock hands, doors, and the slope of inclined surfaces.
